nativeRange.habitat: Intertidal and shallow subtidal hard-bottom zones in brightly lit, high-flow areas

decorPreferences: open water placement or loose attachment to rockwork

avoidWith: aggressive herbivorous fish that may graze it heavily

imageCandidates.notes: Clear specimen shot showing vivid blue coloration under full-spectrum lighting.
Product photo from Top Shelf Aquatics listing.
Product photo from Mosaic Macros listing.

summary: A striking red macroalga with iridescent blue coloration, prized for display tanks. Generally kept under moderate lighting to preserve the blue color; higher light may cause browning.
careNotes:
Hypnea pannosa is a rhodophyte (red alga) in family Cystocloniaceae, notable for its vivid iridescent blue to blue-violet coloration under full-spectrum lighting. The blue appearance results from light reflection rather than fluorescence, so it will not appear blue under actinic-only lighting. Newly arrived specimens often appear grey and tend to color up within a few days once placed under adequate full-spectrum light.

AlgaeBarn recommends moderate-to-high full-spectrum lighting for best coloration and growth; however, many keepers report that excessively high PAR causes the specimen to shift toward reddish-brown. For display-tank color preservation, moderate lighting is generally advised. Top Shelf Aquatics characterizes it as a slow-growing species preferring higher flow.

Growth form is dense, spiky, and highly branched, maturing into loosely attached clumps or mats. No supplemental fertilization is typically needed, though occasional iron and iodine dosing may be beneficial. Generally considered reef-safe and compatible with most reef inhabitants, including snails, hermit crabs, shrimp, and reef fish. The branched structure may harbor hitchhiker organisms from wild-collected stock. Propagation is by simple fragmentation — pieces that detach will form new growth points when placed on rockwork. Per Mosaic care guide guidance: as a red or brown alga, this species will MELT at the base if buried in sand — attach to rock via superglue, zip-tie/fishing line, or wedging instead. (Sand-bed acceptance per Mosaic is limited to Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea genera, plus some calcified Dasycladales.)
breedingNotes: In captivity, propagation is typically achieved through fragmentation: detaching a portion of the thallus and anchoring it to rockwork or rubble. Sexual reproduction (gametophyte-sporophyte alternation of generations) occurs in the wild but has not been documented as a meaningful propagation path in hobby aquariums.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Confirms slow growth rate and preference for higher flow; limited parameter data.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros
additional.notes: Confirms scientific name Hypnea pannosa, iridescent blue coloration mechanism, and grey-on-arrival coloration note. No numeric parameters provided.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
